searchLedger.hypothesis: Non-owner exit does not wipe another live launcher's owner lease

searchActions.result: clearOwnerLeaseFile no-ops unless local ownerLeasePid is set and the current lease ownerPid still matches that local pid at 500-507. clearLeaseFile only removes the PID lease when lease.pid matches this launcher or this launcher's child at 610-615. Secondary bridge-only launchers do not set ownerLeasePid or childProcess.

searchLedger.rationale: The prior bug class was an exit handler deleting a lease still owned by another live launcher. I do not see that specific interleaving in this target.
searchLedger.ruledOutReason: Lease release is conditional on local ownership for the owner lease and on local pid/current child for the PID lease. The remaining lease-deletion risk is the missing release/adoption path covered by the P0 findings, not wiping another live owner's lease.

searchLedger.hypothesis: A single missed live-owner probe is not enough for this file to reap a healthy daemon

searchActions.result: The target delegates live-owner bridge classification to maybeBridgeLeaseHolder at 537-546 and has no direct single-probe reap path in checkStrictSingleWriter.

searchLedger.rationale: The target does not itself reap on the first missed probe. The concrete defect is the opposite: after the delegated bridge reports a respawn decision, the target ignores it.
searchLedger.ruledOutReason: No target-side one-probe healthy-daemon reap path was found.

title: Signal and heartbeat shutdown still kill the shared advisor daemon instead of releasing it
evidence: The signal handler unconditionally sends the signal to childProcess, waits 5s, SIGKILLs, clears all leases, and exits at 914-922; heartbeat loss also kills childProcess and clears leases at 484-490. The child is spawned attached with stdio inherit and no detached/unref at 871-876. The reference has a re-election release branch that does not kill the daemon, removes the exit lease cleaner, clears only owner lease, and exits at mk-spec-memory 1375-1397.
rationale: Interleaving: launcher A owns the shared advisor daemon and launcher B is bridged to A's socket. When A's MCP host disposes the session and SIGTERMs A, A kills the daemon and deletes the lease. B's bridge drops, the host starts another launcher, and the daemon is relaunched instead of preserved for B. This is the same SIGTERM-to-relaunch flap class described in the prompt.

title: Stale launcher lease with a live daemon child is not adopted before respawn
evidence: When leaseHeldFromFile returns staleReclaimable, the target only logs staleReclaimed at 564-566 and continues; leaseHeldFromFile checked only lease.pid liveness at 290-300 and ignores the recorded childPid/socketPath for adoption. Main later writes a new lease and launches a new daemon at 982-993. The reference stale path reads staleLease.childPid, checks it is alive and bridgeable, and bridges instead of reaping/spawning at mk-spec-memory 1498-1514.
rationale: Interleaving: owner launcher dies by SIGKILL or crash after writing a lease with childPid, while the advisor daemon child remains alive and its socket is usable. A fresh launcher sees the owner pid dead, treats the PID lease as stale, never checks childPid/socketPath, overwrites the lease, and starts a second daemon. The warm daemon is neither adopted nor safely reaped.

title: Stale-owner reclaim is not serialized, so two reclaimers can both spawn daemons
evidence: For an existing stale owner lease, acquireOwnerLeaseFile uses non-exclusive writeOwnerLeaseFile plus an immediate reread at 444-454. Inside the bootstrap critical section the target only checks ownsOwnerLeaseFile at 978-982, then launches at 992; it does not re-run the PID lease/adoption check. The reference stale-lease path takes an exclusive respawn lock before reaping or spawning at mk-spec-memory 1515-1526.
rationale: Interleaving: launchers A and B race on the same stale owner lease. A writes/rereads and believes it owns; B then writes/rereads and also believes it owns. A enters the bootstrap lock first, writes the PID lease, and starts a daemon. B later enters, still owns the overwritten owner lease, passes the owner-only check, overwrites the PID lease, and starts another daemon. A's heartbeat may later kill its child, producing a duplicate-writer window and a flap.

title: Deep-probe respawn decisions are ignored by the skill-advisor launcher
evidence: bridgeOrReportLeaseHeld returns maybeBridgeLeaseHolder's decision at 537-546, but checkStrictSingleWriter ignores that decision and returns true in all live-lease branches at 551-554, 559-562, and 573-576. The reference checks decision.action === 'respawn' and calls respawnAfterDeadSocket at mk-spec-memory 839-856.
rationale: Interleaving: a live owner process has a dead or hung socket. The bridge helper can classify the lease holder as needing respawn after its probe attempts, but the target launcher discards that action and exits as though the case was handled. The dead daemon is not reaped or replaced, so reconnecting clients get EOF instead of recovery.

title: Model-server shutdown clears the pid record without killing or escalating the sidecar root
evidence: shutdownModelServerForLauncherExit clears timers, stops the demand listener, calls hfControl.reapProcessTree(pid), and writes a null shared pid at 723-732; this function does not signal the model-server child itself or wait/SIGKILL it. The reference explicitly kills the model-server root and escalates to SIGKILL in the release path at mk-spec-memory 1380-1389 and in normal shutdown at 1415-1429.
rationale: Interleaving: with SPECKIT_SKILL_ADVISOR_MODEL_SERVER_ENABLED=1, the launcher exits while the model server is running. The launcher removes the pid file but can leave the root model-server process alive. A later launcher no longer sees the pid record and may start another sidecar, creating bounded orphan accumulation and socket ownership ambiguity.
